does: This paper introduces contrastive neuron attribution (CNA), a method that identifies the most behaviorally relevant neurons in language models by contrasting activations on harmful versus benign prompts. The approach requires only forward passes without gradients or auxiliary training, pinpointing approximately 0.1% of MLP neurons that most distinguish refusal behavior. When these neurons are ablated in instruction-tuned models, refusal rates decrease by over 50% on standard jailbreak benchmarks while maintaining output fluency and coherence across all intervention strengths. The authors validate findings across multiple model families (Llama and Qwen) and scales (1B to 72B parameters), comparing base and instruct variants to understand how refusal mechanisms develop.
connects: This work builds on prior research in neural network mechanistic interpretability and model steering, extending beyond popular residual stream-based methods like activation addition that suffer from coherence degradation at high intervention strengths. It connects to attribution-based neuron discovery approaches used in understanding model internals, while drawing on the broader literature examining how instruction-tuning shapes model behavior. The paper responds to limitations in understanding where refusal mechanisms localize within models and contributes to ongoing efforts in controlled behavioral modification, building on foundations in mechanistic understanding of language model safety behaviors and the layered structure of semantic and behavioral processing in transformers.
enables: This work opens pathways for more targeted and interpretable model steering approaches that maintain output quality while achieving behavioral changes. The identification of discrete neuron circuits underlying refusal enables future research into understanding the full mechanistic basis of instruction-following and alignment behaviors. The method's efficiency and transparency support development of safer interpretability-based intervention techniques, potentially enabling fine-grained control over specific model behaviors without the quality tradeoffs of existing steering methods. Results across different base and instruct versions suggest promising directions for understanding how alignment training modifies underlying computational structures, enabling future work on alignment robustness, model editing, and designing interventions that respect model coherence constraints.
